preamble: The change from /s/ to /j/ is often a later stage of yod-coalescence, a form of palatalization. Initially, /s/ combines with a following /j/ to form a postalveolar fricative like /ʃ/ (as in 'miss you' becoming 'mish-you'). In some accents, this /ʃ/ can be further lenited and fronted, approaching or becoming a palatal glide /j/.

examples.note: An advanced stage of yod-coalescence where the fricative element is lost.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
